questions.question: The `bash builtins` -u option read input from file descriptor fd instead of the standard input.
questions.explanation: Correct. `bash builtins` -u option read input from file descriptor fd instead of the standard input.

questions.question: The `bash builtins` -u option change and report the soft limit associated with a resource.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. `bash builtins` -u option read input from file descriptor fd instead of the standard input.

questions.question: The `bash builtins` -S option change and report the soft limit associated with a resource.
questions.explanation: Correct. `bash builtins` -S option change and report the soft limit associated with a resource.

questions.question: The `bash builtins` -S option change and report the hard limit associated with a resource.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. `bash builtins` -S option change and report the soft limit associated with a resource.

questions.question: The `bash builtins` -H option change and report the hard limit associated with a resource.
questions.explanation: Correct. `bash builtins` -H option change and report the hard limit associated with a resource.

questions.question: The `bash builtins` -H option report all current limits; no limits are set.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. `bash builtins` -H option change and report the hard limit associated with a resource.

questions.question: The `bash builtins` -a option report all current limits; no limits are set.
questions.explanation: Correct. `bash builtins` -a option report all current limits; no limits are set.

questions.question: The `bash builtins` -a option the maximum socket buffer size.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. `bash builtins` -a option report all current limits; no limits are set.

questions.question: The `bash builtins` -b option the maximum socket buffer size.
questions.explanation: Correct. `bash builtins` -b option the maximum socket buffer size.

questions.question: The `bash builtins` -b option the maximum size of core files created.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. `bash builtins` -b option the maximum socket buffer size.

questions.question: The `bash builtins` -c option the maximum size of core files created.
questions.explanation: Correct. `bash builtins` -c option the maximum size of core files created.

questions.question: The `bash builtins` -c option the maximum size of a process’s data segment.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. `bash builtins` -c option the maximum size of core files created.

questions.question: The `bash builtins` -d option the maximum size of a process’s data segment.
questions.explanation: Correct. `bash builtins` -d option the maximum size of a process’s data segment.

questions.question: The `bash builtins` -d option the maximum scheduling priority ("nice").
questions.explanation: Incorrect. `bash builtins` -d option the maximum size of a process’s data segment.

questions.question: The `bash builtins` -e option the maximum scheduling priority ("nice").
questions.explanation: Correct. `bash builtins` -e option the maximum scheduling priority ("nice").

questions.question: The `bash builtins` -e option the maximum size of files written by the shell and its children.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. `bash builtins` -e option the maximum scheduling priority ("nice").

questions.question: The `bash builtins` -f option the maximum size of files written by the shell and its children.
questions.explanation: Correct. `bash builtins` -f option the maximum size of files written by the shell and its children.

questions.question: The `bash builtins` -f option the maximum number of pending signals.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. `bash builtins` -f option the maximum size of files written by the shell and its children.

questions.question: The `bash builtins` -i option the maximum number of pending signals.
questions.explanation: Correct. `bash builtins` -i option the maximum number of pending signals.

questions.question: The `bash builtins` -i option the maximum number of kqueues that may be allocated.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. `bash builtins` -i option the maximum number of pending signals.

questions.question: The `bash builtins` -k option the maximum number of kqueues that may be allocated.
questions.explanation: Correct. `bash builtins` -k option the maximum number of kqueues that may be allocated.

questions.question: The `bash builtins` -k option the maximum size that may be locked into memory.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. `bash builtins` -k option the maximum number of kqueues that may be allocated.

questions.question: The `bash builtins` -l option the maximum size that may be locked into memory.
questions.explanation: Correct. `bash builtins` -l option the maximum size that may be locked into memory.

questions.question: The `bash builtins` -l option the maximum resident set size (many systems do not honor this limit).
questions.explanation: Incorrect. `bash builtins` -l option the maximum size that may be locked into memory.

questions.question: The `bash builtins` -m option the maximum resident set size (many systems do not honor this limit).
questions.explanation: Correct. `bash builtins` -m option the maximum resident set size (many systems do not honor this limit).

questions.question: The `bash builtins` -m option the maximum number of open file descriptors (most systems do not allow this value to be set).
questions.explanation: Incorrect. `bash builtins` -m option the maximum resident set size (many systems do not honor this limit).

questions.question: The `bash builtins` -n option the maximum number of open file descriptors (most systems do not allow this value to be set).
questions.explanation: Correct. `bash builtins` -n option the maximum number of open file descriptors (most systems do not allow this value to be set).

questions.question: The `bash builtins` -n option the maximum number of bytes in POSIX message queues.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. `bash builtins` -n option the maximum number of open file descriptors (most systems do not allow this value to be set).

questions.question: The `bash builtins` -q option the maximum number of bytes in POSIX message queues.
questions.explanation: Correct. `bash builtins` -q option the maximum number of bytes in POSIX message queues.

questions.question: The `bash builtins` -q option the maximum real-time scheduling priority.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. `bash builtins` -q option the maximum number of bytes in POSIX message queues.
